justification: All 5 expected rules identified (BR-1 through BR-4 as invariant: true, BR-6 weight bracket as invariant: false). State machine directionality correctly modeled — BR-2 explicitly forbids In Transit → Pending, BR-7 enumerates the only valid transitions. 14 acceptance criteria all use Given/When/Then format, far exceeding the minimum of 3. Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable (e.g., 'Creating or updating a shipment with weight <= 0 must be rejected'). Weight bracket correctly marked invariant: false with sound justification. Confidence level of 72 falls within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions. Agent went beyond minimum requirements by adding BR-5 (weight cap as non-invariant) and BR-7 (explicit valid transition graph), plus detailed edge cases and escalations.

observations.text: Correctly split weight rules into BR-4 (weight > 0, invariant: true) and BR-5 (weight <= 1000, invariant: false with business justification) — a nuanced distinction showing domain reasoning.
BR-7 explicitly models the full directed state graph (Pending → In Transit → Delivered only), going beyond just flagging forbidden transitions to defining the complete valid transition set.
Weight bracket (BR-6) correctly classified as invariant: false with clear reasoning: 'Bracket boundaries are pricing policy. Management can adjust thresholds without corrupting system state.'
14 acceptance criteria all in proper Given/When/Then format with concrete preconditions and expected outcomes.
Edge cases are thoughtful — boundary values, floating-point precision, concurrent transitions, and idempotent same-state transitions demonstrate deep domain thinking.
Escalations correctly surface the two key ambiguities (bracket boundary ownership at 10 kg, and Pending → Delivered validity) with options and recommendations.
Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated with clearly separated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions.

BR-5 classifies the 1000 kg weight cap as invariant: false, which is debatable — the rubric expects weight > 0 and <= 1000 as a single invariant: true rule (BR-4 in the rubric). Splitting it and marking the cap as non-invariant is a reasonable domain interpretation but diverges from the expected answer.
